title: Mahamat Idriss Déby Itno
gist: World Bank bio page tracing Chad's president Mahamat Idriss Déby Itno's path from military officer to head of a post-2021 Transitional Military Council, formed after his father's death outside the constitutional line of succession, to elected president in May 2024.

insights.claim: After Marshal Idriss Déby Itno died in April 2021 during military operations in northern Chad, his son Mahamat Idriss Déby Itno was appointed head of a Transitional Military Council after the President of the National Assembly declined to assume the constitutional interim presidency.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A de facto military succession that bypassed the formal constitutional line is a political-risk marker for Chad, relevant to sovereign creditworthiness and investment-climate assessments.
insights.locator: Following the death of Marshal Idriss Déby Itno in April 2021

insights.claim: Mahamat Idriss Déby Itno was elected President of the Republic of Chad in May 2024 following a presidential election, converting a roughly three-year military transition into an electoral mandate that he has held since.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: The electoral conversion of a military transition is a legitimacy marker worth tracking for Chad's standing with donors and regional partners.
insights.locator: In May 2024, following a presidential election

insights.claim: Before becoming president, Mahamat Idriss Déby Itno rose through the presidential guard and reached the rank of General of the Army in 2021, and led Chadian Armed Forces operations in Mali, for which he received official national recognition.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Chad's military leadership has direct operational ties to Sahel security operations (Mali), relevant to regional security-risk mapping.
insights.locator: He has also distinguished himself on the international stage
